planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed; an indeterminate variety that needs staking or caging despite its compact clusters of small-medium fruit.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before last frost in a warm, moist seed-starting mix kept at 70–85°F; transplant deeply after frost danger has passed, burying the stem up to the lowest leaves to encourage a strong root system. Tigerella is a British heirloom also sold under the name Mr. Stripey in the UK, producing heavy clusters of 2–3 ounce fruits with vivid red skin and irregular gold-yellow stripes and a tangy, balanced flavor that is excellent fresh or in sauces. Stake or cage plants early and begin harvesting when the fruit is firm but fully colored at the blossom end; unlike many striped types it ripens evenly and does not crack easily. Regular irrigation helps maintain even soil moisture and reduces blossom-end rot.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; a true black/brown-type beefsteak related to Cherokee Purple, with darker, more chocolatey skin and flesh coloration; stake or cage firmly to support large fruits.

directions: Start seed indoors at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant into deeply prepared, rich soil after all frost risk has passed; bury the stem deeply to develop a robust root system. Cherokee Chocolate is a sport or selection from Cherokee Purple with even darker, mahogany-brown skin and deep chocolate-red flesh, yielding large 10–16 ounce beefsteak fruits with an outstandingly rich, complex, slightly smoky flavor prized by growers who love the dark-fruited heirlooms. Harvest when fruit softens slightly at the blossom end since the unusual dark coloring makes it difficult to judge ripeness by color alone; shoulder color transitions from green to brownish rather than the typical red flush. Excellent raw or in salads where the dramatic interior color makes a striking visual impact.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; an indeterminate orange beefsteak with deep-meaty flesh and very small seed cavities; plant early in warm climates to get harvest before summer heat peaks.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to a rich, well-drained site in full sun after frost danger has passed, burying the stem to the lowest leaves. Kellogg's Breakfast is an American heirloom orange beefsteak tomato that originated with William Kellogg of Michigan, introduced to the larger seed-saving community through Seed Savers Exchange, and has since become one of the most celebrated orange heirlooms for its extraordinary flavor — sweet, mild, and low-acid with a rich, fruity depth that makes it ideal fresh. Fruits weigh 1–2 pounds and have very meaty, almost seedless flesh that is deep orange throughout; they ripen relatively slowly but the wait is well worth it. Stake or cage plants firmly and thin to two or three main leaders for the largest fruits.
sources: Heirloom Tomato Growers Guide, Eastern Michigan University Extension

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; a green-when-ripe beefsteak that ripens to a luminous chartreuse-green with a slight yellow or pink blush at the blossom end — judge ripeness by softening and a color shift rather than relying on a typical red stage.

directions: Sow seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after frost danger has passed, planting the stem deeply in rich, well-drained soil and staking firmly from the start. Aunt Ruby's German Green is a family heirloom introduced to Seed Savers Exchange in the 1990s and named for Ruby Arnold of Tennessee; it produces enormous, sometimes 1-pound beefsteak fruits that remain green when fully ripe, turning only slightly yellow-chartreuse with a pinkish blush at the base — a striking and unusual appearance at the table. The flesh is deep green, extremely juicy, spicy-sweet, and complex, with a flavor that stands apart from any red beefsteak; it is among the most flavorful of all green tomatoes. Harvest promptly when the blossom-end softens, as green fruits left too long become mealy.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into deeply prepared soil after all frost risk passes; this is an indeterminate variety that needs staking or caging to support the heavy orange beefsteak fruits.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost, transplant into fertile, well-draining soil in full sun, and bury the stem deeply for a stronger root system. Dr. Wyche's Orange honors Dr. John Wyche, a Cherokee chief and circus impresario from Hugo, Oklahoma, who grew tomatoes fertilized with circus animal manure and shared his seeds with Seed Savers Exchange; the variety produces large, up to 2-pound, brilliant orange beefsteak fruits with very meaty, nearly seedless flesh and a mild, sweet, low-acid flavor that appeals especially to those who find red tomatoes too acidic. Stake or cage plants solidly as the vines are vigorous and the fruits are heavy; consistent watering prevents cracking. Harvest when fully orange and slightly soft at the blossom end.
